question: When coupling twin trailers, the shut-off valves at the rear of the front trailer should be:
explanation: The shut-off valves at the rear of the front trailer (and at the rear of the dolly) should be open to allow air to reach the brakes on the second trailer.

question: When coupling doubles or triples, the trailer height should be:
options: Slightly lower than the center of the fifth wheel
Higher than the center of the fifth wheel
correct_answer: Slightly lower than the center of the fifth wheel
explanation: The trailer should be slightly lower than the center of the fifth wheel so that when you back under it, the trailer will be lifted slightly as the fifth wheel locks into place.

question: The converter dolly air tank drain valve should be:
options: There is no drain valve on a dolly
On the side of the dolly
At the front of the dolly
At the rear of the dolly
correct_answer: At the rear of the dolly
explanation: The converter dolly air tank drain valve is located at the rear of the dolly and should be drained regularly to remove moisture and contaminants.

question: When backing a trailer, you should:
options: Turn the steering wheel in the direction you want the trailer to go
Turn the steering wheel in the opposite direction you want the trailer to go
correct_answer: Turn the steering wheel in the opposite direction you want the trailer to go
explanation: When backing a trailer, turn the steering wheel in the opposite direction you want the trailer to go. This is opposite of backing a car.

question: The air leakage rate for a combination vehicle with the engine off and brakes applied should not exceed:
explanation: For a combination vehicle (tractor-trailer), the air loss rate with the engine off and brakes applied should not exceed 4 psi in one minute.

question: Before coupling, the fifth wheel should be:
options: Tilted down toward the rear of the tractor
Tilted up toward the rear of the tractor
correct_answer: Tilted down toward the rear of the tractor
explanation: The fifth wheel should be tilted down toward the rear of the tractor so that when you back under the trailer, the kingpin will slide smoothly into the fifth wheel.

question: When coupling doubles, after connecting the converter dolly to the front trailer, the dolly support should be:
explanation: After connecting the converter dolly to the front trailer, raise the dolly support to the up position to allow the dolly to move freely.

question: The pintle hook on the rear of the first trailer should be:
explanation: The pintle hook (or coupling device) on the rear of the first trailer must be properly latched and locked to securely connect to the converter dolly.

question: Doubles and triples are more likely to turn over than other vehicles because:
options: The rear trailer tends to swing out in a turn (crack the whip effect)
correct_answer: The rear trailer tends to swing out in a turn (crack the whip effect)
explanation: In doubles and triples, the rear trailer can swing out sharply in turns, especially if you turn too quickly. This 'crack the whip' effect can cause rollovers.

question: When inspecting doubles or triples, make sure the shut-off valves:
options: At the rear of the last trailer are open, and all others are closed
At the rear of the last trailer are closed, and all others are open
correct_answer: At the rear of the last trailer are closed, and all others are open
explanation: All shut-off valves should be open except the ones at the rear of the last trailer, which should be closed to maintain air pressure in the system.

question: The trailer air supply control should be used to:
options: Release the trailer brakes for normal driving
Both supply air and release the trailer brakes
Supply air to the trailer brakes
Apply the trailer brakes in an emergency
correct_answer: Both supply air and release the trailer brakes
explanation: The trailer air supply control (also called the tractor protection valve control) supplies air to the trailer brakes and releases the trailer emergency brakes for normal driving.

question: When coupling, after backing under the trailer, you should:
options: Check that the kingpin is locked into the fifth wheel
correct_answer: Check that the kingpin is locked into the fifth wheel
explanation: After backing under the trailer, check that the kingpin is properly locked into the fifth wheel before proceeding. This ensures a secure connection.

question: The service line (also called the control line or signal line) carries:
options: Air to control the trailer brakes
correct_answer: Air to control the trailer brakes
explanation: The service line (blue) carries air controlled by the brake pedal to apply the trailer brakes during normal driving.

question: The emergency line (also called the supply line) is:
explanation: The emergency line (supply line) is red. It supplies air to the trailer air tanks and controls the emergency brakes on the trailer.

question: Before uncoupling, you should:
explanation: Before uncoupling, lower the landing gear to support the trailer. Make sure it is fully extended and the crank handle is secured.

question: When pulling doubles or triples, you should:
options: Use cruise control at all times
Allow more space for stopping and turning
correct_answer: Allow more space for stopping and turning
explanation: Doubles and triples require more space for stopping and turning. Allow extra following distance and take turns more slowly to prevent rollovers.

question: A converter dolly is used to:
options: Support the front of the second or third trailer
Connect the tractor to the first trailer
correct_answer: Support the front of the second or third trailer
explanation: A converter dolly (or dolly) is a coupling device with one or two axles and a fifth wheel used to support the front of a second or third trailer.

question: When coupling twin trailers, you should secure the second trailer by connecting the:
options: Converter dolly to the rear of the first trailer
correct_answer: Converter dolly to the rear of the first trailer
explanation: Secure the second trailer by properly connecting the converter dolly to the rear of the first trailer using the pintle hook or coupling device.

question: The maximum length for a double trailer combination on most highways is:
explanation: Maximum length limits for double and triple trailers vary by state. Check state regulations before operating in a new state.

question: When coupling, after connecting the air lines, you should:
options: Charge the trailer air system and check for leaks
correct_answer: Charge the trailer air system and check for leaks
explanation: After connecting the air lines, charge the trailer air system by pushing in the trailer air supply knob. Then check for air leaks before driving.

question: The glad hands are used to:
options: Connect the air lines between the tractor and trailer
correct_answer: Connect the air lines between the tractor and trailer
explanation: Glad hands are coupling devices used to connect the service and emergency air lines between the tractor and trailer or between trailers.

question: When driving doubles or triples, you should check the coupling devices:
options: Before the trip and during the trip
Only when you feel a problem
correct_answer: Before the trip and during the trip
explanation: Check all coupling devices before the trip and regularly during the trip to ensure they remain secure and properly connected.

question: The safety chains or cables should be:
options: Crossed under the tongue of the dolly
correct_answer: Crossed under the tongue of the dolly
explanation: Safety chains or cables should be crossed under the tongue of the dolly to provide support if the coupling fails.

question: When uncoupling twin trailers, you should:
options: Disconnect both at the same time
explanation: When uncoupling twin trailers, disconnect the rear trailer first, then the converter dolly, and finally the front trailer.

question: Rearward amplification (crack the whip effect) is:
options: Not a concern for experienced drivers
Only a problem in single trailers
More pronounced in longer combination vehicles
correct_answer: More pronounced in longer combination vehicles
explanation: Rearward amplification (crack the whip effect) is more pronounced in longer combination vehicles. The rear trailer can swing out much more than the tractor during turns or lane changes.

question: When pulling more than one trailer, which trailer should be the heaviest?
options: The first trailer behind the tractor
correct_answer: The first trailer behind the tractor
explanation: The heaviest trailer should always be in the first position behind the tractor. The lightest trailer should be in the rear.

question: Which of these is NOT a good thing to do when driving on slippery roads?
options: Use the engine brake or retarder
correct_answer: Use the engine brake or retarder
explanation: Do not use the engine brake or retarder on slippery roads, as it can cause the drive wheels to skid and lead to a jackknife.

question: You should check the height of the trailer before coupling because:
options: It ensures the kingpin is aligned
If it is too high, the tractor may hit the trailer nose
It prevents damage to the landing gear
If it is too low, the tractor may not be able to back under it
correct_answer: If it is too high, the tractor may hit the trailer nose
explanation: If the trailer is too high, the tractor may back under it without engaging the kingpin, potentially hitting the trailer nose or cab.

question: When uncoupling the rear trailer, you should lower the landing gear until it:
options: Lifts the trailer off the dolly
Makes firm contact with the ground
correct_answer: Makes firm contact with the ground
explanation: Lower the landing gear until it makes firm contact with the ground and supports the trailer weight.

question: After coupling the converter dolly to the front trailer, you should:
options: Connect the air lines and electrical cable
explanation: After coupling, ensure the pintle hook is locked, safety chains are connected, and all air and electrical lines are properly attached.

question: The best way to stop a skid is to:
options: Steer in the direction of the skid
correct_answer: Steer in the direction of the skid
explanation: To correct a skid, take your foot off the brake and steer in the direction you want the vehicle to go (into the skid).

question: When driving a set of doubles, you must be careful to avoid:
explanation: Sudden steering movements can cause the rear trailers to swing out and potentially roll over (crack the whip effect).

question: If you must brake on a slippery surface, you should use:
explanation: Use controlled or stab braking to stop on slippery surfaces without locking the wheels and losing steering control.

question: Which of these statements about managing space is true for doubles/triples?
options: Space management is the same as a car
You need more space to stop and turn than with a single trailer
You need less space to stop
You can turn sharper than a single trailer
correct_answer: You need more space to stop and turn than with a single trailer
explanation: Doubles and triples are longer and heavier, requiring more distance to stop and wider paths for turning.

question: When inspecting the converter dolly, you should check for:
explanation: Inspect the dolly thoroughly for structural damage, loose parts, and air leaks to ensure it is safe to use.

question: The 'crack the whip' effect is most likely to tip over:
explanation: The rear trailer is the most susceptible to the 'crack the whip' effect and is the most likely to roll over during sudden movements.

question: When connecting the converter dolly to the rear trailer, you should:
options: Back the dolly under the rear trailer
Drive the rear trailer onto the dolly
correct_answer: Back the dolly under the rear trailer
explanation: You must back the converter dolly under the rear trailer to couple them. This requires skill and practice.

question: If you cannot avoid backing a double trailer combination, you should:
options: Back up as fast as possible
Disconnect the rear trailer and back them separately
Back up slowly and correct often
correct_answer: Disconnect the rear trailer and back them separately
explanation: It is extremely difficult and dangerous to back double trailers. If possible, disconnect the rear trailer and back the units separately.

question: When checking the air brakes on a double trailer combination, you should:
options: Only check the front trailer brakes
Check air flow to all trailers
Assume they work if the light is off
correct_answer: Check air flow to all trailers
explanation: You must ensure that air flows through the entire system to the rear of the last trailer to ensure all brakes are working.

question: Which valve supplies air to the converter dolly?
options: Both the emergency and service lines
explanation: The emergency line (supply line) fills the air tanks on the converter dolly and the trailers.

question: You should test the trailer brakes by:
options: Pulling the hand valve and driving gently
correct_answer: Pulling the hand valve and driving gently
explanation: Test trailer brakes by pulling the hand valve (trolley valve) and trying to move the vehicle gently against the brakes.

question: When driving doubles, you should look at least _____ seconds ahead.
explanation: Good drivers look 12 to 15 seconds ahead to anticipate hazards and avoid sudden maneuvers.

question: If the converter dolly has a light, it is usually:
explanation: Converter dollies manufactured after 1998 are required to have an amber lamp on the left side.

question: To uncouple the rear trailer, you must first:
explanation: Always park on level ground, apply parking brakes, and chock wheels before uncoupling to prevent the trailer from rolling.

question: When coupling, the safety catch for the fifth wheel locking lever must be:
explanation: The safety catch must be properly positioned over the locking lever to prevent the fifth wheel from opening accidentally.

question: Empty trucks:
options: Are safer to drive in wind
Have worse traction and longer stopping distances
correct_answer: Have worse traction and longer stopping distances
explanation: Empty trucks have less weight on the tires, reducing traction and often increasing stopping distance due to bouncing and wheel lockup.

question: Which of these is a danger of 'bobtailing' (driving tractor only)?
explanation: Bobtail tractors can be harder to stop (especially in rain), easier to skid, and have more sensitive steering.

question: When connecting the converter dolly, the air lines should be:
options: Connected glad hand to glad hand (same color)
correct_answer: Connected glad hand to glad hand (same color)
explanation: Always connect red to red (emergency) and blue to blue (service) to ensure proper brake function.

question: If you must make a sudden stop, you should:
explanation: Use controlled braking to stop as quickly as possible without locking the wheels and losing control.

question: Rollovers are more likely when:
explanation: Speed, high center of gravity, and sudden movements all contribute to the risk of rollovers.

question: When driving at night, you should:
options: Drive slower so you can stop within your headlight range
correct_answer: Drive slower so you can stop within your headlight range
explanation: You should never overdrive your headlights; always be able to stop within the distance you can see.

question: The 'off-tracking' of the rear wheels causes them to:
options: Follow a tighter path than the front wheels
Follow a wider path than the front wheels
correct_answer: Follow a tighter path than the front wheels
explanation: Rear wheels follow a tighter path (cut the corner) than the front wheels, known as off-tracking or mistracking.

question: When making a right turn with doubles, you should:
options: Turn wide as you complete the turn
Keep the rear of the trailer close to the curb
Swing wide to the left before the turn
correct_answer: Turn wide as you complete the turn
explanation: Keep the rear close to the curb initially, but turn wide as you complete the turn to prevent the rear trailer from hitting the curb or other objects.

question: If you have a breakdown, you should place reflective triangles:
options: On the roof of the cab
explanation: Standard placement is 10 feet, 100 feet, and 200 feet behind the vehicle on a highway to warn approaching traffic.

question: When checking the coupling of the converter dolly, ensure:
options: There is no space between the upper and lower fifth wheel
The locking jaws are around the shank of the kingpin
explanation: A secure coupling requires no gap, locked jaws, and a secured release arm.

question: Which trailer is most likely to jackknife?
explanation: Empty trailers have less traction and are more prone to skidding and jackknifing.

question: Antilock Braking Systems (ABS) help you:
options: Maintain steering control during hard braking
correct_answer: Maintain steering control during hard braking
explanation: ABS prevents wheel lockup, allowing you to maintain steering control while braking hard.

question: If a trailer begins to skid, you should:
explanation: Releasing the brakes allows the wheels to start rolling again and regain traction.

question: When connecting the electrical cable, ensure:
options: It has enough slack for turns
explanation: The electrical cable must be secure, undamaged, and have enough slack to prevent unplugging during turns.

question: The total weight of the combination vehicle must not exceed:
explanation: Never exceed the GCWR specified by the manufacturer or legal limits.

question: When driving in strong winds, doubles and triples are:
options: Less stable and prone to tipping
correct_answer: Less stable and prone to tipping
explanation: The large surface area and multiple pivot points make doubles and triples very susceptible to strong winds.

question: To prevent trailer skid, you should:
explanation: Smooth, early braking reduces the chance of wheel lockup and skidding.

question: When coupling, the landing gear of the second trailer should be:
explanation: Always fully raise the landing gear after coupling to prevent it from catching on the ground.

question: A 'shut-off valve' is located:
options: At the rear of each trailer
correct_answer: At the rear of each trailer
explanation: Shut-off valves are at the rear of trailers to open or close the air lines for the next trailer.

question: When pulling triples, the heaviest trailer should be:
explanation: Always put the heaviest trailer first to improve stability.

question: The 'pintle hook' connects:
options: The dolly to the lead trailer
correct_answer: The dolly to the lead trailer
explanation: The pintle hook on the rear of the lead trailer connects to the eye of the converter dolly.

question: Safety chains should have:
explanation: Chains need enough slack to allow the vehicle to turn, but not so much that they drag.

question: When checking the dolly air tank, ensure:
explanation: The tank must be secure, sealed, and leak-free.

question: If you experience a trailer jackknife, you should:
explanation: Releasing the brakes allows the trailer wheels to grip the road again.

question: Doubles and triples need more space because:
explanation: Length, weight, and articulation all increase the space needed for maneuvers.

question: When parking doubles, try to park:
options: So you can pull forward when leaving
correct_answer: So you can pull forward when leaving
explanation: Avoid backing whenever possible; plan your parking so you can drive forward to exit.

question: The converter dolly has its own:
explanation: The dolly has an air tank to supply its brakes.

question: When inspecting tires on doubles, check for:
explanation: Tire health is critical for safe operation.

question: You should check your mirrors:
explanation: Frequent mirror checks are essential to monitor the trailers and traffic.

question: If a tire blows out, you should:
explanation: Maintain control, avoid braking which could cause a spin, and let the vehicle slow naturally.

question: When connecting the converter dolly, the pintle hook must be:
explanation: The hook must be securely closed and latched to hold the dolly eye.

question: To check if the converter dolly is secure, pull gently against the:
explanation: With trailer brakes applied, pull gently to ensure the coupling holds.

question: When uncoupling, you should disconnect the safety chains:
explanation: Ensure the trailer is supported before removing safety connections.

question: The 'eye' of the converter dolly connects to:
explanation: The tow bar eye connects to the pintle hook on the lead trailer.

question: When driving doubles, you should increase your following distance to:
options: One second for every 10 feet of vehicle length
correct_answer: One second for every 10 feet of vehicle length
explanation: Plus add one second if going over 40 mph.

question: Which of these is true about converter dollies?
options: They rely on the tractor brakes only
explanation: Dollies are equipped with brakes and air tanks.

question: When coupling, the dolly should be lined up:
options: Directly in front of the second trailer
correct_answer: Directly in front of the second trailer
explanation: Proper alignment ensures the kingpin engages the fifth wheel correctly.

question: If the dolly has a support leg, it should be:
explanation: Raise the support leg so it doesn't drag or hit obstacles.

question: When checking the air system, the compressor should build pressure from 85 to 100 psi within:
explanation: In modern systems, pressure should build quickly to ensure safety.

question: The emergency line on the dolly should be connected to:
options: The emergency line on the rear of the lead trailer
correct_answer: The emergency line on the rear of the lead trailer
explanation: Connect matching lines to supply air to the dolly.

question: When uncoupling, you should chock the wheels of:
explanation: Prevent the trailer from rolling when disconnected.

question: You should check for loose lugs on:
explanation: Loose lugs can lead to wheel loss; check every wheel.

question: If the second trailer does not have spring brakes, you should:

question: When driving doubles, you should avoid:
explanation: Frequent lane changes increase the risk of the 'crack the whip' effect and instability.

correct_answer: Use a low gear and light, steady braking pressure
explanation: Snub braking (apply to 5 mph below safe speed, release, repeat) or steady light pressure with proper gearing is best.

question: When driving doubles, you should look at least _____ seconds ahead. (Standard)
explanation: Good drivers look 12 to 15 seconds ahead to anticipate hazards and avoid sudden maneuvers.

question: If the converter dolly has a light, it is usually: (Standard)
explanation: Converter dollies manufactured after 1998 are required to have an amber lamp on the left side.

question: To uncouple the rear trailer, you must first: (Standard)
explanation: Always park on level ground, apply parking brakes, and chock wheels before uncoupling to prevent the trailer from rolling.

question: When coupling, the safety catch for the fifth wheel locking lever must be: (Standard)
explanation: The safety catch must be properly positioned over the locking lever to prevent the fifth wheel from opening accidentally.

question: Empty trucks: (Standard)
options: Are safer to drive in wind
Have worse traction and longer stopping distances
correct_answer: Have worse traction and longer stopping distances
explanation: Empty trucks have less weight on the tires, reducing traction and often increasing stopping distance due to bouncing and wheel lockup.

question: Which of these is a danger of 'bobtailing' (driving tractor only)? (Standard)
explanation: Bobtail tractors can be harder to stop (especially in rain), easier to skid, and have more sensitive steering.

question: When connecting the converter dolly, the air lines should be: (Standard)
options: Connected glad hand to glad hand (same color)
correct_answer: Connected glad hand to glad hand (same color)
explanation: Always connect red to red (emergency) and blue to blue (service) to ensure proper brake function.
